text.title: Privacy Regulations, IP Protection, and Contract Essentials
text.summary: Key privacy regulations matrix, IP type priorities, and minimum contract set for a new online business.

rule.statement: Determine which regulations apply based on user geography, not company location: GDPR applies if you have EU users; CCPA applies if you have California users; CAN-SPAM applies to all US email marketing.
rule.rationale: Regulation follows the user, not the business address. A US LLC serving EU users must comply with GDPR.

list.item: GDPR (EU users): consent for non-essential cookies, data processing agreement with vendors, respond to access/deletion requests within 30 days, report breaches within 72 hours
CCPA/CPRA (California users): disclose data collection, provide opt-out of sale, honor access and deletion requests
CAN-SPAM (US email): no deceptive subject lines, include physical address, working unsubscribe link, honor opt-outs within 10 days
COPPA (children under 13): parental consent required before collecting any data; avoid targeting if product may attract minors

section.title: Intellectual Property

rule.statement: Before launch, search the USPTO TESS database for your company name, product name, and tagline. Register the company name trademark as the first priority.
rule.rationale: Trademark rights in the US attach to first use, but registration is required to sue for infringement. A name collision discovered post-launch requires rebranding, which costs far more than a $350 trademark filing.

section.p: IP type priority order: (1) company name trademark, (2) product name, (3) logo, (4) tagline if distinctive. Copyright on creative works is automatic — registration is optional but required to sue. Trade secrets require only consistent internal secrecy; no registration.
section.reference: USPTO trademark status lookup stub via TSDR API

rule.statement: Run a pre-launch name check: USPTO TESS search, domain availability, social media handle availability. Do all three before committing to a brand name.
rule.rationale: Acquiring a domain after launch from a squatter costs 10–100x registration price. Social handle conflicts require awkward alternatives.

section.title: Contract Essentials

rule.statement: Every contractor agreement must include: "work for hire" IP assignment clause, moral-rights waiver where jurisdiction allows, confidentiality clause, independent-contractor status statement, and payment terms.
rule.rationale: Without IP assignment, the contractor may retain copyright on work product. Without independent-contractor language, "employee-style" controls (set hours, supplied equipment) create misclassification liability.

section.p: Minimum contract set: customer agreement (scope, payment, liability), contractor agreement (IP assignment, confidentiality, IC status), NDA (for sharing confidential information with partners), affiliate agreement (commission structure, disclosure requirements, approved messaging).

rule.statement: Include severability and entire-agreement clauses in every contract.
rule.rationale: Severability prevents one unenforceable clause from voiding the whole contract. Entire-agreement prevents verbal side-deals from becoming enforceable obligations.
